text: . (37) As before, we need to avoid resonant terms on the right-hand side. These are terms proportional to cos ( τ + ϕ ) and sin ( τ + ϕ ). Some terms of this form already appear explicitly in (37) . But—and this is the important point—there is also a resonant term lurking in sin ( τ + ϕ ) cos 2 ( τ + ϕ ), because of the trigonometric identity sin ( τ + ϕ ) cos 2 ( τ + ϕ ) = 1 4 [ sin ( τ + ϕ ) + sin 3 ( τ + ϕ ) ] . (38) (Exercise 7.6.10 reminds you how to derive such identities, but usually we won’t need them—shortcuts are available, as we’ll see.) After substituting (38) into (37) , we get ∂ τ τ x 1 + x 1 = [ − 2 r ′ + r − 1 4 r 3 ] sin ( τ + ϕ ) + [ − 2 r ϕ ′ ] cos ( τ + ϕ ) − 1 4 r 3 sin 3 ( τ + ϕ ) . (39) To avoid secular terms, we require − 2 r ′ + r − 1 4 r 3 = 0 (40) − 2 r ϕ ′ = 0. (41) First consider (40) . It may be rewritten as a vector field r ′ = 1 8 r ( 4 − r 2 ) (42) on the half-line r ≥ 0. Following the methods of Chapter 2 or Example 7.1.1, we see that r * = 0 is an unstable fixed point and r * = 2 is a stable fixed point. Hence r ( T ) → 2 as T → ∞. Secondly, (41) implies ϕ ′ = 0, so ϕ ( T ) = ϕ 0 for some constant ϕ 0 . Hence x 0 ( τ , T ) → 2cos ( τ + ϕ 0 ) and therefore x ( t ) → 2 cos ( t + ϕ 0 ) + O ( ε ) (43) as t → oo. Thus x ( t ) approaches a stable limit cycle of radius = 2 + O ( ε ). To find the frequency implied by (43) , let θ = t + ϕ ( T ) denote the argument of the cosine. Then the angular frequency ω is given by ω = d θ d t = 1 + d ϕ d T d T d t = 1 + ε ϕ ′ = 1 , (44) through first order in ε . Hence ω = 1 + O ( ε 2 ); if we want an explicit formula for this O ( ε 2 ) correction term, we’d need to introduce a super-slow time ℑ = ε 2 t , or we could use the Poincaré-Lindstedt method, as discussed in the exercises. ■ Averaged Equations The same steps occur again and again in problems about weakly nonlinear oscillators. We can save time by deriving some general formulas. Consider the equation for a general weakly nonlinear oscillator: x ¨ + x + ε h ( x , x ˙ ) = 0. (45) The usual two-timing substitutions give O ( 1 ) : ∂ τ τ x 0 + x 0 = 0 (46) O ( ε ) : ∂ τ τ x 1 + x 1 = − 2 ∂ τ T x 0 − h (47) where now h = h ( x 0 , ∂ τ x 0 ). As in Example 7.6.2, the solution of the O (1) equation is x 0 = r ( T ) cos ( τ + ϕ ( T ) ) . (48) Our goal is to derive differential equations for r ′ and ϕ′, analogous to (40) and (41) . We’ll find these equations by insisting, as usual, that there be no terms proportional to cos ( τ + ϕ ) and sin ( τ + ϕ ) on the right-hand side of (47) . Substituting (48) into (47) , we see that this right-hand side is 2 [ r ′ sin ( τ + ϕ ) + r ϕ ′ cos ( t + ϕ ) ] − h (49) where now h = h ( r cos ( τ + ϕ ), − r sin ( τ + ϕ )). To extract the terms in h proportional to cos ( τ + ϕ ) and sin ( τ + ϕ ), we borrow some ideas from Fourier analysis. (If you’re unfamiliar with Fourier analysis, don’t worry—we’ll derive all that we need in Exercise 7.6.12.) Notice that h is a 2 π -periodic function of τ + ϕ . Let θ = τ + ϕ . Fourier analysis tells us that h (θ) can be written as a Fourier series h ( θ ) = ∑ k = 0 ∞ a k cos k θ + ∑ k = 1 ∞ b k sin k θ (50) where the Fourier coefficients are given by a 0 = 1 2 π ∫ 0 2 π h ( θ ) d θ a k = 1 π ∫ 0 2 π h ( θ ) cos   k θ   d θ , k ≥ 1 a k = 1 π ∫ 0 2 π h ( θ ) sin   k θ   d θ , k ≥ 1. (51) Hence (49) becomes 2 [ r ′ sin θ + r ϕ ′ cos θ ] − ∑ k = 0 ∞ a k cos k θ − ∑ k = 1 ∞ b k sin k θ . (52) The only resonant terms in (52) are [ 2 r ′ - b 1 ] sin θ and [ 2 rϕ ′ − a 1 ] cos θ.
